pursuitEconomics.content:
Compensation & Opportunity Assessment

Series A at $61M funding with ~37 employees suggests significant equity upside potential. Early-stage PM roles typically offer $150–220K base + 0.3–0.8% equity (assuming ~200M shares outstanding post-Series A). Giga's AI customer support niche has strong tailwinds—enterprise support automation is undergoing rapid consolidation. Growth trajectory matters more than current cash comp. Risk: runway-dependent; Series B timing and outcome will determine liquidation probability.

  • Equity incentive: Strong for next 4–6 years, contingent on Series B success
  • Base comp: Expect $160–190K range for SF-based PM role
  • Market timing: AI support solutions at inflection; first-mover PM advantage if execution succeeds

existence.content:
Why This Role Exists Now

Giga is expanding post-Series A and moving beyond initial product-market fit. Early PM founder/CTO likely wearing too many hats; Series A capital unlocks dedicated PM to own roadmap, customer research, and metric-driven iteration. Customer support automation is exploding—vendors like Zendesk, Intercom, and legacy providers are fighting against AI-native entrants. Giga needs a seasoned PM to lock in initial customer cohort, establish product differentiation, and build repeatable go-to-market before Series B fundraising (~18 months).

  • Why now: Series A capital + market tailwinds demand dedicated product focus
  • Scope: Likely owning core product roadmap, customer research, metrics
  • Growth stage: PMF validation phase transitioning to scale

companyMarket.content:
Company Market & Positioning

Giga operates in the $10B+ global customer support software market. AI customer support is the fastest-growing sub-segment; Intercom, Zendesk, and traditional helpdesk vendors are all racing to embed generative AI. Giga likely differentiates on simplicity, agent-in-the-loop design, or domain-specific accuracy. Enterprise customers expect sub-second response times, multi-channel integration (email, chat, phone), and low hallucination rates. Giga's addressable market spans mid-market SaaS (500–5K employees) to enterprise. Competitive threats include Intercom, Zendesk's AI, and niche players like Custom Ink and Forethought.

  • TAM: $10B+ global support software; AI is fastest-growing segment
  • Key buyers: VP Support, CX leaders at mid-market to enterprise
  • Differentiation risk: Space is crowded; product must solve a specific pain (accuracy, speed, integration)

plausible.content:
Realistic Requirements & Expectations

Giga will seek a PM with SaaS go-to-market experience, ideally in developer tools, customer support, or enterprise software. Likelihood of finding someone with AI customer support domain expertise is low; they'll prioritize product thinking, metric discipline, and ability to deep-dive customer conversations. Preferred background includes: shipping products in B2B SaaS, owning P&L or core metric (retention, NRR, LTV), and comfort with technical products and cross-functional leadership (engineering + sales). AI/ML platform familiarity is a strong plus but not required if the candidate has built in adjacent spaces (e.g., automation, workflow tools).

  • Must-haves: SaaS product experience, metric ownership, customer research skill
  • Nice-to-haves: AI/ML product intuition, support software domain knowledge, go-to-market leadership
  • Culture fit: Scrappy, execution-focused mindset; comfort with limited process and ambiguity

screenOut.content:
Disqualification Risks & Red Flags

Giga will screen out candidates who lack hands-on product leadership experience or show discomfort with early-stage chaos. Key disqualifiers: minimal shipped product track record, heavy reliance on design/research teams (Giga is too lean), no SaaS revenue exposure, or unclear customer empathy for support teams. High salary expectations ($250K+) misaligned with Series A equity stage may signal over-leveled candidate. Candidates uncomfortable with technical products or API integrations, or those with primarily consumer product backgrounds, may struggle. Founder compatibility is critical in Series A; candidates with perceived "better-than-thou" attitude risk cultural rejection.

  • Experience gaps: Consumer-only background, minimal shipping track record
  • Structural misalignment: Salary expectations >> Series A budget
  • Support domain gaps: No exposure to B2B ops, enterprise sales motion, or customer support workflows
  • Team fit: Inability to operate autonomously or adapt to founder direction

needs.content:
What Giga Really Needs (Beneath the Job Description)

Giga's hidden need is a PM who can validate/refine their initial customer thesis before Series B. Are they building for SMB support teams, enterprise CX centers, or developer integrations? The founding team likely has conviction but needs external validation and disciplined customer research to lock in positioning. They need someone to own NRR, churn analysis, and win/loss insights—metrics that will directly influence Series B fundraising. Internally, they need someone to bridge between engineering and go-to-market, translating customer feedback into prioritized roadmap without requiring constant founder review. They also need confidence that the PM can articulate product strategy to potential VCs and lead investor conversations as the company scales.

  • Real need #1: Deep customer insight; refine ICP before hitting series B funding crunch
  • Real need #2: Metric ownership (NRR, expansion revenue, churn analysis)
  • Real need #3: Bridge between engineering and go-to-market; reduce founder PM burden
  • Real need #4: VC-ready narrative and ability to lead investor conversations

fit.content:
Ili's Fit: Strengths & Natural Advantages

Ili's 15+ years in ad tech, data/ML platforms, and consumer + B2B SaaS map directly to Giga's needs. Ad tech PM experience translates to SaaS metrics discipline, data-driven prioritization, and customer-obsessed iteration. ML platform background ensures comfort with AI/ML product nuance, algorithm evaluation, and technical cross-functional leadership. The combination of consumer product (scale, engagement loops) and B2B SaaS (sales, retention, contract value) gives Ili a rare vantage point for evaluating Giga's positioning strategy. Ili's ability to own metrics and customer research aligns with Giga's core need for data-driven decision-making at Series A. Early-stage SaaS experience (likely via ad tech or startup involvement) demonstrates scrappiness, execution bias, and comfort with founder dynamics.

  • ML/data platform intuition: Direct match for AI customer support product category
  • SaaS metrics expertise: Proven track record owning NRR, churn, and expansion revenue
  • Customer research depth: Ad tech background requires obsessive customer understanding
  • Cross-functional leadership: ML platform work demands engineering + go-to-market collaboration

gaps.content:
Gaps & Mitigation Strategies

Ili's primary gap is likely no direct customer support or contact center domain expertise. Mitigate by: (1) scheduling 4–5 pre-interviews with support leaders at Giga's target customers to demonstrate learning velocity, (2) reading 2–3 support industry benchmarks (Forrester, Gartner) to show sector familiarity, (3) speaking with Giga's founding team about their customer thesis and stress-testing it with informed questions. A second gap may be limited Series A founder dynamics experience if Ili's career was primarily large-company SaaS. Mitigation: Speak to early-stage SaaS mentors, ask founders for unscripted feedback loops, and emphasize previous experience influencing without authority (likely from ad tech or cross-functional matrix organizations). Brand equity from prior roles also helps—Ili's track record at established firms signals reduced execution risk to founders navigating capital constraints.

  • Gap #1—Support domain: Mitigate via pre-interview customer research, sector reading
  • Gap #2—Founder dynamics: Mitigate via mentorship, transparency about learning curve, track record of influence
  • Mitigation strategy: Lead with metric discipline and customer research prowess; position domain knowledge as learnable

network.content:
Network Strategy & Warm Introductions

Identify warm intros via: (1) former colleagues from ad tech or SaaS companies who have invested in or joined Series A/B startups, (2) customer success or support leaders at Ili's prior companies who may know Giga's early customers, (3) venture capital partners focusing on AI/automation/support tech (Sequoia, Greylock, Lerer Hippeau) who have Giga in portfolio or can warm-intro to founders, (4) alumni networks from MBA or prior employer if Giga founders attended same institutions. Most powerful move: reach out to 3–5 support leaders or CX heads from companies Ili knows (e.g., Stripe, Shopify, HubSpot) and ask if they've evaluated Giga; get their perspective on product fit, then reference these conversations in founder outreach. This proves customer empathy and insider market perspective.

  • Highest-leverage intros: VCs with Giga in portfolio; founders' MBA/prior employer networks
  • Secondary paths: Support/CX leaders from Ili's network; customer referenceable introductions
  • Proof point: Name 2–3 specific customers evaluating Giga; show market insight

interview.content:
Interview Preparation & Key Narratives

Prepare to discuss: (1) Ili's experience owning a key metric (NRR, churn, LTV) and iterating based on customer data—emphasize scrappiness, testing velocity, and ability to make decisions with incomplete info, (2) a time Ili influenced engineering or sales teams without direct authority, showing cross-functional leadership in high-stakes environments, (3) how Ili thinks about AI/ML product—not the math, but the user experience implications, hallucination risks, and pricing/packaging strategy for AI-native products. Expect founders to pressure-test Ili's go-to-market experience: have a crisp thesis on Giga's ICP and land-and-expand strategy ready. Bring 3–5 thoughtful questions about customer conversations, competitive positioning, and product-market fit signals that show sector homework. Avoid appearing over-prepared or consultant-like; Giga values scrappy operators, not polished decks.

  • Story #1: Metric ownership + iteration velocity in constrained environments
  • Story #2: Cross-functional influence; overcoming resistance to drive key decision
  • Story #3: AI/ML product thinking; pricing, UX, and positioning strategy insight
  • Key Q to ask: "What signal would tell you ICP hypothesis is wrong? How would you pivot?"

proofPoints.content:
Proof Points & Credibility Anchors

Ili should lead with: (1) Named customer logos and contract expansion metrics from prior SaaS role (e.g., "Grew NRR from 105% to 118% over 18 months by implementing X customer research cadence"), (2) Shipping speed and iteration examples—specific features shipped, time-to-decision, A/B tests run—that demonstrate execution discipline, (3) ML platform product intuition from prior role (e.g., "Led pricing strategy for data platform serving 500+ customers; differentiated on latency and accuracy, not raw feature count"), (4) Cross-functional team leadership—names of engineering leaders, sales VPs, data scientists Ili influenced—showing ability to earn credibility quickly, (5) Customer empathy stories: specific support interactions, pain points observed, or win/loss insights that shaped product decisions. Bring 1–2 recommendation letters or references from prior founders/executives for speed of trust-building.

  • Quantified wins: NRR improvement, churn reduction, ARR expansion tied to PM decisions
  • Shipping velocity: Feature launch timeline, iteration speed, decision-making examples
  • Domain fluency: ML product strategy, pricing/packaging for AI-native products
  • Social proof: Customer references, founder relationships, VC connections

dealBreaker.content:
Potential Deal Breakers & Why It Might Not Work

Key risks: (1) Giga's founders may have rigid product vision; if Ili's customer research contradicts their thesis, founder friction escalates quickly in early stage. Assess founder openness to evidence-based pivots in first conversation, (2) AI customer support remains a competitive dunking ground; if Giga's differentiation is unclear or weakens post-hire, Ili could be inherited a sinking ship despite strong PM execution, (3) Series A runway constraints mean limited hiring, budget, and compensation growth—if Ili values rapid team-building or significant salary growth, misalignment emerges quickly, (4) Founder equity math may not work if Series A option pool is small or strike price is high; ensure financial advisors review offer before commitment, (5) Market consolidation risk: if Zendesk, Intercom, or well-funded competitors aggressively acquire Giga's customers, top-down market pressure could crush company irrespective of PM skill.

  • Founder friction risk: Assess openness to data-driven change in founder conversations
  • Market consolidation: Research incumbent move speed; track Series B fundraising for competitive startups
  • Runway/compensation: Confirm equity math with legal/financial advisor; understand Series B timeline
  • Differentiation clarity: If founders cannot articulate clear product advantage, red flag on sustainability

nextSteps.content:
Action Items for This Week

By Wednesday: (1) Identify 2–3 warm intro paths to Giga founders via LinkedIn/investor networks, (2) Schedule 4 customer conversations with support/CX leaders at Ili's current/prior companies; learn their Giga evaluation, (3) Draft 1-page positioning doc: Ili's thesis on Series A PM role, how Ili would think about ICP/go-to-market in first 90 days. By Friday: (4) Secure warm intro to founder or hiring contact; send brief, metrics-forward email, (5) Complete 2–3 sector deep-dives (Gartner, Forrester support software reports), (6) List 5–7 thoughtful questions on Giga's customer thesis, competitive positioning, and product roadmap priorities. Next week: (7) First coffee call with founder—focus on understanding customer pain and founder vision, (8) Request customer reference calls post-initial meeting to show continued customer research investment.

  • Customer research: 4 conversations this week to build sector credibility
  • Positioning: 1-pager on 90-day product strategy; show metric discipline from day one
  • Warm intro: Execute by Friday; lead with customer insight, not just credentials
